channel.title: Darkhorse Press - Top Story
channel.description: Latest Top Story posts from Darkhorse Press.

item.title: Two men face charges after shooting reported on I-20 near Hwy. 18 in Jackson - Darkhorse Press
item.author: Morgan Howard
item.description:
JACKSON — Two men have been charged in connection with a shooting on Interstate 20 near Highway 18 appeared before a municipal court judge Tuesday, according to the Jackson Police Department.

Police said 32-year-old Alejandro Macedo received a $50,000 bond, including $25,000 for aggravated assault and $25,000 for shooting into an occupied vehicle. Authorities said Macedo was also ordered to wear an ankle monitor.

Police said 32-year-old Omar Nerio received a $750,000 bond, including $500,000 for aggravated assault and $250,000 for shooting into an occupied vehicle.

The shooting was reported around 6 a.m. Feb. 10. Police said officers were directed to meet the victim at I-20 and Springridge Road in Clinton. Detectives with the department’s Robbery/Homicide Unit responded and began an investigation.

Investigators said the victim was traveling westbound on I-20 near Highway 18 in a white Freightliner semi-tractor truck when he attempted to change lanes. Police said the driver of another 18-wheeler pulled alongside the victim’s vehicle and tried to prevent the lane change. Authorities said the drivers argued, and the passenger in the second truck then pointed a weapon and fired several shots into the victim’s truck.

Police said the victim was struck once in the shoulder and was taken to a local hospital for treatment of a non-life-threatening injury.

Detectives later identified the driver and passenger of the suspect vehicle as Macedo and Nerio, police said. Warrants were obtained for their arrests.

Jackson police said they worked with the Grand Prairie Police Department in Grand Prairie, Texas, to locate and arrest the suspects and obtain a search warrant for the vehicle. Both men were charged with aggravated assault and shooting into an occupied vehicle.

Police said the suspects remain in custody in Texas awaiting extradition back to Jackson.

The case remains under investigation. Anyone with information is asked to contact the Jackson Police Department at 601-960-1234 or Crime Stoppers at 601-355-TIPS (8477).

item.title: Ridgeland Police Looking to Identify Individual Seen in Photo - Darkhorse Press
item.author: Morgan Howard
item.description:
RIDGELAND- The Ridgeland police department is asking the public to help identify a person seen in a photo.

There are no details as to why they want to identify her.

If you recognize her, please contact the Ridgeland Police Department Criminal Investigations Division at 601-856-2121.

There are no details as to why they want to identify her.

If you recognize her, please contact the Ridgeland Police Department Criminal Investigations Division at 601-856-2121.

item.title: Rankin County man arrested in fish house burglary - Darkhorse Press
item.author: Morgan Howard
item.description:
RANKIN COUNTY – The Rankin County Sheriff’s Office has arrested a suspect in connection with a burglary at a fish house on Dixie Road in the Florence area.

Deputies received a report of a commercial burglary on Feb. 24 after employees discovered cash missing from the register when they opened the business for the day.

A review of security footage showed a male suspect wearing a black hoodie walking near the rear of the restaurant the previous night, according to the sheriff’s office. Investigators said the suspect went to the electrical box and turned off the breaker, cutting power to the restaurant. Additional surveillance cameras in the area also captured images of the suspect.

Deputies began investigating and identified and arrested the suspect in less than 24 hours.

The suspect was identified as Scott Dennis Roberts of Rankin County. He was booked into the Rankin County Jail on a charge of burglary.

The Rankin County District Attorney’s Office will bring Roberts before County Court Judge David Morrow for an initial appearance.

Sheriff Bryan Bailey thanked the deputies involved in the case and said the department will continue working to keep Rankin County safe.

item.title: Operation County Sweep Nets 26 Drug Arrests in Pearl River County; Dozens Still Sought - Darkhorse Press
item.author: Therese Apel
item.description:
PEARL RIVER COUNTY — A countywide narcotics roundup led by the Pearl River County Sheriff’s Office resulted in 26 arrests, with dozens more suspects still at large, authorities said.

The operation, dubbed “Operation County Sweep,” involved narcotics investigators and deputies with the sheriff’s office, along with the Picayune Police Department, Picayune Special Operations Team, Mississippi Bureau of Narcotics, Federal Bureau of Investigation, Mississippi Department of Corrections, Lenoir Rowell Criminal Justice Center Transport and Picayune Municipal Court.

Authorities said the agencies traversed the entirety of Pearl River County to serve narcotics-related warrants. The operation yielded 26 arrests on charges related to possessing and distributing illegal narcotics and other offenses.

Those arrested include:

Authorities said the following individuals remain at large with active warrants:

• Tina Walters (36) – Poplarville, MS 39470. Charged with Sale of a Controlled Substance. • Harold “HL” Taylor (38) – Lumberton, MS 39455. Charged with Possession of a Controlled Substance. • Dillon Warren (31) – Lumberton, MS 39455. Charged with Sale of Controlled Substance. • James Mayes (54) – Lumberton, MS 39455. Charged with the Sale of a Controlled Substance. • Willie Jefferson (39) – Carriere, MS 39426. Charged with 4 counts for Sale of Controlled Substance. • Douglas Dillard (49) – Pearl River, LA 70452. Charged with 3 counts Sale of Controlled Substance. • Blake Bass (30) – Picayune, MS 39466. Charged with 4 counts for the Sale of a Controlled Substance. • Candi Summers (45) – Carriere, MS 39426. Charged with Possession of a Controlled Substance. • Steven Summers (26) – Carriere, MS 39426. Charged with Possession of a Controlled Substance. • Jalishia Hunderson (UNK) – Carriere, MS 39426. Charged with 2 counts of the Sale of a Controlled Substance. • Matthew Blackburn (27) – Carriere, MS 39426. Charged with the Sale of a Controlled Substance. • Lainey Bethe (19) 26230 Broad Ridge Dr. Picayune, MS 39466. Charged with Possession of a Controlled Substance. • Daramie Magee (29) – Picayune, MS 39466. Charged with 3 counts for the Sale of a Controlled Substance. • Senica Nixon (41) – Picayune, MS 39466. Charged with 3 counts for Trafficking a Controlled Substance and 1 charge for the Sale of a Controlled Substance. • Tarik Ragas (21) – Picayune, MS 39466. Charged with 3 counts for the Sale of a Controlled Substance. • Madyson Mitchell (28) – Picayune, MS 39466. Charged with the Sale of a Controlled Substance. • Martin Minks (40) – Picayune, MS 39466. Charged with Possession of a Controlled Substance and Trafficking a Controlled Substance. • Nathan Nix (23) – Picayune, MS 39466. Charged with the Sale of a Controlled Substance.

The Picayune Police Department Special Operations Team is also seeking the following individuals:

• Sylvester McDonald (49) – Picayune, MS 39466. Charged with 2 counts of Conspiracy to Sell a Controlled Substance. • James Lavigne (50) – Picayune, MS 39466. Charged with Conspiracy to Sell a Controlled Substance. • Brady Joseph Ervin (21) – Picayune, MS 39466. Charged with Conspiracy to Sell a Controlled Substance. • Tasha Lauren Stackpole (43) – Picayune, MS 39466. Charged with Possession of a Weapon by a Felon. • Christian Daniel Lee (31) – Picayune, MS 39466. Charged with 3 counts of Sale of a Controlled Substance.

Anyone with information on the at-large individuals is asked to contact Central Dispatch at 601-749-5482.

Sheriff David Allison commended narcotics investigators and deputies for their work and thanked state, federal and local partners for their assistance.

“This operation was only the beginning,” Allison said, adding that narcotics activity will not be tolerated in Pearl River County.

Mississippi Department of Public Safety Commissioner Sean Tindell said the operation demonstrates the strength of interagency partnerships.

“Multiple agencies came together with one goal — to remove criminals from our streets and protect Mississippi families and communities,” Tindell said. “If you’re trafficking drugs in our state, know this: we are coming for you.”

item.title: MSU announces new graduate programs in One Health - Darkhorse Press
item.author: Special to Darkhorse Press
item.description:
STARKVILLE — Mississippi State, a longtime leader in One Health principles, now is offering two new graduate programs focusing on this public health approach that recognizes the interconnectedness of animal, human and environmental wellbeing.

Beginning this fall, MSU’s College of Veterinary Medicine is offering One Health graduate programming, a Master of Science with two distinct tracts and a certificate for professionals looking to expand their knowledge and skillsets. The options are in collaboration with the College of Agriculture and Life Sciences and College of Forest Resources.

One Health is an interdisciplinary and scientifically holistic field promoting collaboration across medicine, veterinary science, environmental science and related disciplines to better detect, prevent and respond to health threats on a local and global scale.

“One Health is a viewpoint. It’s a lens that we are trying to help our students see through so they can be better leaders for the future,” said CVM One Health Director and Associate Professor Dr. Nicole Ashpole.

An expert in neuroscience and toxicology who joined the university to lead the program last year, Ashpole has been working with an interdisciplinary team to build the new curricula. The master’s program will prepare students to tackle issues like zoonotic disease emergence, antimicrobial resistance and accelerated loss of biodiversity. A non-thesis track is a series of engaging courses designed for completion in one year that introduces key foundational concepts in data science and epidemiology, disease management, systems thinking, policy and leadership. The thesis track also is available for students who wish to pursue larger research projects under direction of One Health research professors.

The abbreviated four-course certificate program is available through MSU’s Center for Distance Education.

“At Mississippi State University, we have veterinarians, we have forestry resource specialists, agricultural specialists, agricultural scientists, social scientists, people working in the humanities, and people leading our leadership institutions,” said Ashpole. “We are perfectly positioned to bring all these people together to have our students and trainees learn from experts across all these disciplines to make a better tomorrow.”

Ashpole explained MSU has been at the forefront of One Health since the concept was coined following a series of zoonotic disease outbreaks in the early 2000s. Longtime CVM Professor Dr. Margaret Khaitsa used the approach when building the ongoing Tropical Veterinary and One Health summer study abroad collaboration between CVM and Uganda’s Makerere University.

“We bring not only veterinary students. We also bring students from other disciplines,” Khaitsa said. “They work together with their fellow students from Uganda who themselves are from different disciplines.”

Now under the leadership of Assistant Professor of Pathobiology and Population Medicine Dr. Stephen Reichley, CVM students and professors spend a month each summer in the African country, working across disciplines to experience the philosophy and application of One Health in the field.

“Because of the program Dr. Khaitsa founded, our experience with One Health at this university is vast. The fact that you have Mississippi students learning from Ugandan students and vice-versa is the very heart of One Health because our students get to bring that knowledge about animal production and wildlife disease surveillance back with them,” Ashpole said.

In 2021, Khaitsa was named to the One Health High-Level Expert Panel, which advises the Food and Agriculture Organization of the United Nations, the World Organization for Animal Health, United Nations Environment Program, and World Health Organization.

Grounded in MSU’s land-grant mission, the Division of Agriculture, Forestry, and Veterinary Medicine has worked for generations to improve the health and resilience of people, animals and the environment across the Magnolia State and beyond. The new One Health programs build on this legacy by preparing students to apply integrated thinking and strategic problem-solving to global health challenges.

For more information about the MSU College of Veterinary Medicine’s One Health graduate programming, visit www.vetmed.msstate.edu/onehealth .

item.title: Malik Reed gets 70 years in McComb park shooting that killed 6-year-old - Darkhorse Press
item.author: Therese Apel
item.description:
MCCOMB — Malik Reed was sentenced Monday to 70 years in prison, with the first 50 years to serve, after pleading guilty to second-degree murder and four counts of aggravated assault in a 2022 shooting at a McComb park that left a 6-year-old boy dead and four others injured.

Reed entered the guilty plea Feb. 12 in the Circuit Court of Pike County. Judge David Strong imposed the sentence Monday, ordering Reed to serve the time in the custody of the Mississippi Department of Corrections.

The charges stem from a shooting at Central Park in McComb, where multiple people were struck by gunfire near the park’s basketball courts and playground.

According to authorities and previous reporting by The Enterprise-Journal, shots were fired from a black Dodge Charger traveling east on Minnesota Avenue. Police said the vehicle had bullet holes, indicating someone may have fired back. Several nearby homes were also struck by gunfire.

The 6-year-old victim died of his injuries at Southwest Mississippi Regional Medical Center. A 12-year-old who was shot was airlifted, and three other victims, ages 23, 18 and 17, were also injured.

District Attorney Brendon Adams said the sentence should send a message.

“The actions of the defendant in this case will not be tolerated in our communities,” Adams said in a statement. “Another example of our children in the communities resorting to gun violence and everyone loses. We will not tolerate this type of senseless violence.”

Adams said he hopes the sentence will serve as a deterrent and bring some measure of closure to the victims’ families after four years.

The case was prosecuted by Adams and Assistant District Attorney Rodney Tidwell.

Reed, along with Bryan Cameron, Yajari Jackson and Bryceon Thompson, had initially been charged with capital murder and four counts of aggravated assault manifesting extreme indifference to human life.

item.title: Lincoln County man gets 40 years on drug and gun charges - Darkhorse Press
item.author: Therese Apel
item.description:
BROOKHAVEN — A Lincoln County jury on Friday found Austin Pierre Jackson guilty of multiple drug trafficking and firearm charges, and a judge sentenced him to 40 years in prison, with the first 23 years to serve.

Jackson was convicted in the Circuit Court of Lincoln County of possession of a firearm by a convicted felon; possession of at least 10 but less than 30 grams of fentanyl with intent to distribute; possession of at least 10 but less than 30 grams of cocaine with intent to distribute; and possession of fewer than 10 dosage units of amphetamine with intent to distribute. All counts were enhanced by possession of a firearm.

Judge David Strong ordered Jackson to serve the sentence in the custody of the Mississippi Department of Corrections.

The case stemmed from a law enforcement detail conducted in Bogue Chitto involving the Lincoln County Sheriff’s Office and the Mississippi Highway Patrol. Authorities said a traffic stop and subsequent search of Jackson’s vehicle led to the seizure of the narcotics.

District Attorney Brendon Adams said the sentence sends a clear message.

“We will not tolerate drug dealers that continue to bring this poison into our communities,” Adams said in a statement. “Those that bring these illegal narcotics into our community and distribute to our people and children will be dealt with harshly.”

Adams thanked the Lincoln County Sheriff’s Office and the Mississippi Highway Patrol for their work in the investigation and said his office considers the safety of families, schools, churches and law enforcement a top priority.

Assistant District Attorney Steven Waldrup prosecuted the case.

Assistant District Attorney Steven Waldrup prosecuted the case.

item.title: Jackson Public Schools hosts Black History Month block party at Two Mississippi Museums - Darkhorse Press
item.author: Morgan Howard
item.description:
JACKSON — More than 1,000 Jackson Public Schools students and their families gathered Feb. 21 for “Legacy on the Block: Jackson’s Day of Cultural Legacy Block Party” at the Two Mississippi Museums, according to Jackson Public Schools.

The event featured activities for students and families, including screenings of “Xavier Riddle and the Secret Museum” from PBS Kids, trackless train rides, inflatables, T-shirt making and food from local vendors. Students also received vouchers for free children’s meals from participating food trucks.

District officials said the event was part of a national effort focused on teaching, protecting and celebrating Black history across generations. Organizers said the goal was to invest in cultural spaces and transform museums into interactive learning environments where students can connect with history.

Jackson Public Schools partnered with Fahrenheit Creative Group and Color of Change to host the event.

City of Jackson and Hinds County officials, along with members of the Mississippi Legislative Black Caucus, were also acknowledged for their support, according to the district.

item.title: Firefighters assist Macedonia VFD, later respond to Rawls Springs fire - Darkhorse Press
item.author: Morgan Howard
item.description:
NORTH FORREST – Firefighters with the North Forrest Volunteer Fire Department responded to two mutual aid structure fires Tuesday evening, according to the department.

At approximately 6 p.m. on Feb. 24, units were dispatched to the area of Morriston Road and Shady Oak Lane in the Macedonia Volunteer Fire Department’s jurisdiction for a reported structure fire.

Upon arrival, crews reported heavy fire coming from a mobile home. Firefighters assisted with a defensive attack and remained on scene for nearly two hours before the fire was brought under control. No injuries were reported.

Later, at approximately 10 p.m., crews responded to the 100 block of Creek Lane in the Rawls Springs community for another reported structure fire.

Firefighters arrived to find a single-story, wood-framed house with fire venting through the roof. Crews made an aggressive interior attack and quickly brought the fire under control, officials said.

No injuries were reported in either incident.

At approximately 6 p.m. on Feb. 24, units were dispatched to the area of Morriston Road and Shady Oak Lane in the Macedonia Volunteer Fire Department’s jurisdiction for a reported structure fire.

Upon arrival, crews reported heavy fire coming from a mobile home. Firefighters assisted with a defensive attack and remained on scene for nearly two hours before the fire was brought under control. No injuries were reported.

Later, at approximately 10 p.m., crews responded to the 100 block of Creek Lane in the Rawls Springs community for another reported structure fire.

Firefighters arrived to find a single-story, wood-framed house with fire venting through the roof. Crews made an aggressive interior attack and quickly brought the fire under control, officials said.

No injuries were reported in either incident.

item.title: Dog with severe mange rescued outside funeral home in Sardis - Darkhorse Press
item.author: Morgan Howard
item.description:
SARDIS – The Tunica Humane Society says it has rescued a dog that had been reported at a funeral home in Sardis and is now asking the public for donations as the animal receives emergency veterinary care.

In a social media post, the organization said the dog, now named “Beacon,” was rescued after volunteers spent hours trying to get him into a trap. The humane society thanked two men it described as “boots on the ground” in Sardis for helping with the rescue.

The organization said Beacon’s condition was more serious than expected and asked supporters to pray for his recovery.

In an update, the Tunica Humane Society said Beacon is currently at a veterinarian receiving emergency treatment and that officials are optimistic about his future, though they said he faces a long and costly recovery.

According to the organization, Beacon has severe mange with secondary infection, along with heartworms and internal parasites. The humane society said he is also anemic.

The group said it is committed to providing the senior dog with ongoing care and is accepting donations to help cover medical expenses. Donations can be made through the Tunica Humane Society’s online payment options listed on its social media pages, or by mailing checks to P.O. Box 236, Tunica, MS 38676.

item.title: Texas fugutive arrested after found lying in grass near Mississippi highway - Darkhorse Press
item.author: Morgan Howard
item.description:
ADAMS COUNTY – A Texas fugitive was arrested Monday afternoon after deputies responded to a welfare check call along U.S. Highway 61 North, according to the Adams County Sheriff’s Office.

Deputies were dispatched at approximately 4:05 p.m. Feb. 23 to 648 Highway 61 North after receiving a report of an individual lying in the grass near the highway.

Upon arrival, deputies made contact with a man later identified as Jessip Shawn Fontenot. Authorities said Fontenot gave inconsistent statements about his identity and his reason for being in Natchez. Deputies also observed what appeared to be dried blood on his clothing, which he was unable to clearly explain.

A routine check through the National Crime Information Center revealed Fontenot had an active felony warrant for probation violation related to burglary of a building from the Bowie County Sheriff’s Office in Texarkana, Texas. Bowie County authorities confirmed the warrant and advised they would extradite.

Fontenot was taken into custody without incident and transported to the Adams County Sheriff’s Office. He will be held pending extradition to Texas.

The Adams County Sheriff’s Office said it remains committed to public safety and assisting partner agencies in apprehending wanted fugitives.

item.title: State auditor says MDE failed to publish required evaluations of afterschool programs - Darkhorse Press
item.author: Morgan Howard
item.description:
The Mississippi Department of Education has failed for years to publish federally required evaluations of its afterschool programs, according to a new analysis from the Mississippi Office of the State Auditor.

The report found that millions of federal dollars continue to flow from MDE to nonprofits and school districts to operate afterschool programs, even though required statewide evaluations have not been made public since November 2022.

In fiscal year 2024, nonprofits and school districts analyzed in the report spent more than $11 million in Nita M. Lowey 21st Century Community Learning Centers grant funds to provide afterschool care to students.

Under federal requirements, subgrantees must collect and submit performance data to MDE. The department is then required to use that data to complete an annual statewide program evaluation and submit it to the federal government. According to the auditor’s office, MDE has not published the required report in more than two years.

“As we have seen on the news in Minnesota and here at home during the welfare scandal, making sure that these taxpayer funds are monitored is critical,” State Auditor Shad White said in a statement. “We will continue to highlight any weaknesses we see in the programs providing money for afterschool or childcare centers.”

The auditor’s office also highlighted additional concerns in the report. Analysts said some subgrantees may have violated state and federal regulations by hiring external evaluators who were not independent. In one instance, a grantee hired an individual who worked for the nonprofit arm of the organization being evaluated, creating a potential conflict of interest.

The report also noted that attendance data for the centers grouped student participation in multi-day and multi-hour increments. As a result, a student who attended one day or only a few hours each month could be counted in the same attendance category as a student with perfect attendance.

The report is the second in a series examining taxpayer-funded childcare programs in Mississippi.

The full report is available on the State Auditor’s website under the “Reports” tab by searching “afterschool.”

item.title: Pearl police searching for missing 16-year-old last seen Tuesday - Darkhorse Press
item.author: Morgan Howard
item.description:
PEARL — The Pearl Police Department is asking for the public’s help in locating a missing teenager who was last seen Tuesday.

Police said Cameron D’undre Primus, 16, was last seen February 24 in Pearl around The Highlands Trailer Park.

Primus is described as a Black male, about 5 feet 5 inches tall and weighing approximately 160 pounds. He was last seen wearing a white T-shirt with red writing and black pants.

Anyone with information about his whereabouts is asked to contact the Pearl Police Department at 601-939-7000.

item.author: Morgan Howard
item.description:
PASCAGOULA – The Pascagoula Police Department is asking for the public’s help in locating a missing runaway teen.

Police said Melody Chandler was last seen leaving her residence in the area of Ingalls Avenue on Feb. 22, 2026.

Chandler is described as 5 feet 4 inches tall, weighing approximately 200 pounds, with purple or auburn-colored hair.

Anyone with information about her whereabouts is asked to contact the Pascagoula Police Department at 228-762-2211.

item.title: MS Blood Services Offers Chance to win $1,000 Visa Gift Card if you donate blood - Darkhorse Press
item.author: Morgan Howard
item.description:
JACKSON- Mississippi Blood Services needs blood donations. From February 24- February 28, if you donate, you will be given a $20 Visa gift card.

You will also automatically be entered to win a $1000 Visa gift card or one of two $500 prizes.

The locations are:

• Flowood: 115 Tree Street, Flowood, MS • Cleveland: 609 N. Davis Ave., Suite 101B, Cleveland, MS • Oxford: 2627 W. Oxford Loop, Suite C, Oxford, MS

Here are the hours for each clinic during February 24–27:

Feb. 24–27 • Flowood: – Feb. 24: 9:00 AM – 5:00 PM – Feb. 25 & 26: 9:00 AM – 5:30 PM – Feb. 27: 9:00 AM – 5:00 PM • Cleveland: 9:00 AM – 5:00 PM • Oxford: 9:00 AM – 5:00 PM

Feb. 28 – All Centers Open • Flowood: 7:30 AM – 2:30 PM • Cleveland & Oxford: 9:00 AM – 2:30 PM

Walk ins are welcome at all clinics and appointments are encouraged.

Remember, giving blood literally saves lives!

item.title: Lawrence County Animal Shelter in desperate need of dry dog food - Darkhorse Press
item.author: Morgan Howard
item.description:
LAWRENCE COUNTY – A Lawrence County animal shelter called Paws of Love is asking for the community to help them with dry dog food.

The shelter said they are completely out of dry food and still have some wet food cans for the animals in their care.

They said they take any brand of dry dog food but love the dry kibble at Rushing Ag LLC.

If you can help with even one bag, it would make a huge difference to the rescue.

We all need to step up and care for the forgotten animals in the community.

item.title: GoFundMe Launched for Newton Restaurant Owner Battling Cancer - Darkhorse Press
item.author: Therese Apel
item.description:
NEWTON — A GoFundMe campaign has been launched to support Newton restaurant owner Ronnie Cook after he was diagnosed with stage 4 pancreatic cancer and is unable to work during treatment.

Cook, owner of Cook’s BBQ & Seafood in Newton , has stepped away from the restaurant as he undergoes treatment, with family members saying the exhaustion caused by the illness has made it impossible for him to continue working.

Supporters say the diagnosis has brought both physical and financial challenges as Cook begins intensive cancer treatment.

“Fighting pancreatic cancer like this can be draining and can be extremely expensive,” organizers wrote on the fundraising page. “We would love to be able to take some of the burden off of him and his family during this time so they can focus on treatment and not stress about money.”

Cook is described by family and friends as a devoted son, brother, husband, father, grandfather and friend who has long served the Newton community through his restaurant.

Organizers said donations of any amount are appreciated and asked for prayers for Cook and his family as he battles the disease.

The GoFundMe page is accepting online donations to help cover medical and living expenses during his treatment.

item.title: George County man arrested for allegedly sexually abusing two minor children - Darkhorse Press
item.author: Morgan Howard
item.description:
GEORGE COUNTY- A Lucedale man was arrested after the George County Sheriff’s Office received a report alleging the sexual abuse of two children under the age of 18.

Following an investigation by the Criminal Investigations Division, warrants for Sexual Battery and Molestation – Touching of a Child for Lustful Purpose were issued for B. Kyler Mallette, 27, of Lucedale.

Today, February 24, investigators served both warrants at his house. Mallette was taken into custody without incident and is currently incarcerated at the George County Regional Correctional Facility awaiting bond.

“We take every allegation involving children seriously. Our investigators worked diligently to ensure this case was handled thoroughly and responsibly, and we remain committed to seeking justice for victims.”

This is an ongoing investigation.

item.title: Fulton Man Arrested After Deputies Find Meth, Gun During Traffic Stop - Darkhorse Press
item.author: Therese Apel
item.description:
FULTON — An Itawamba County man was arrested Feb. 18 after deputies discovered a felony amount of methamphetamine and a firearm during a traffic stop on Highway 178, authorities said.

Deputies with the Itawamba County Sheriff’s Department stopped a vehicle in Fulton and identified the driver as Jerrick Scott Keys of Fulton.

During the stop, deputies located what they described as a felonious amount of methamphetamine and a firearm inside the vehicle. An agent with the North Mississippi Narcotics Unit responded to the scene and assisted with a search of the vehicle, where an additional bag of methamphetamine was found, officials said.

Keys was taken into custody and transported to the Itawamba County Jail. Deputies reported that as Keys exited the patrol vehicle, they observed more methamphetamine on the floorboard where he had been seated.

While Keys was being processed into the jail, detention staff located a metal container on his person that also contained methamphetamine, authorities said.

Keys was charged with possession of a felony amount of methamphetamine, enhanced with a firearm, and tampering with evidence. His bond was set at $60,000.

item.title: First State Bank Welcomes Hurd and Skinner to Quitman Branch - Darkhorse Press
item.author: Therese Apel
item.description:
QUITMAN — First State Bank has expanded its lending team in Quitman, Miss. with the addition of Stephanie Hurd as assistant vice president and branch manager as well as Lowery Skinner as consumer lender. The new hires reflect the bank’s continued commitment to business growth and community development in Clarke County.

Hurd brings extensive banking experience and strong community ties to her new role. Previously, she served in multiple positions at Magnolia State Bank in Heidelberg, Miss., including branch manager, loan officer, and loan assistant. A graduate of Quitman High School, she earned her Bachelor of Business Administration from Belhaven University and associate degrees from Pearl River Community College.

“I am honored to return to my hometown and work alongside our customers,” Hurd said. “I look forward to helping grow Quitman’s businesses and being a part of its bright future.”

Lowery Skinner, a native of Waynesboro, Miss., earned a Bachelor of Business Administration in finance from Mississippi State University’s College of Business. He is also a graduate of Jones College in Ellisville, Miss. Since joining First State Bank part-time in 2023, he has served as a loan operations assistant, credit analyst and management trainee. His experience across departments provides a strong foundation for serving consumer lending needs in the Quitman market.

“Stephanie and Lowery embody the core values of First State Bank,” said Darron Dodd, executive vice president of retail banking. “Their experience and dedication will play an important role in strengthening relationships and supporting economic growth throughout Clarke County.”

First State Bank’s Quitman branch will host an open house reception on Thursday, March 5, 2026, at 310 S. Archusa Ave. in Quitman. The event will celebrate the new additions to the team, and all community members are invited to attend from 12:00 p.m. to 2:00 p.m. for light hors d’oeuvres.

Founded in 1934, First State Bank remains committed to meeting the financial needs of area individuals and businesses, all while providing dedicated customer service. Headquartered in Waynesboro, Miss., the organization has locations in Buckatunna, Clara, Ellisville, Laurel, Leakesville, Lucedale, Gluckstadt, Meridian, Quitman and State Line.

For additional information on First State Bank, visit www.firststatebnk.bank .

item.title: Final week to donate corn for March of the Mayors food drive in Pearl - Darkhorse Press
item.author: Morgan Howard
item.description:
PEARL – Residents have until Feb. 27 to donate canned corn as part of the March of the Mayors Extra Table Feeds food drive in Pearl.

City officials said this is the final week for donations, with canned corn being collected to help support families in need throughout the community.

Organizers emphasized that every donation contributes to putting food on the table for local families. Residents are encouraged to purchase extra cans during routine grocery trips and drop them off at any City of Pearl location listed on the city’s promotional materials.

The deadline to donate is Thursday, Feb. 27.

item.title: Dr. RaShall Brackney adds a personal touch to JPD leadership, promises joy in the job - Darkhorse Press
item.author: Therese Apel
item.description:
JACKSON — On Tuesday, just hours before going before the Jackson City Council to be confirmed as Jackson’s new police chief, Dr. RaShall Brackney met with members of the police department at Precinct Four, and her message was one of encouragement.

She gave answers to questions previous administrations had waved off, or answered summarily with a no, and rather than a bureaucratic, “We’ll look into that,” her answers were more along the lines of, “Oh, that’s easy.”

Under previous administrations, reimbursement for related education was just something that was promised, but it was never given. The answer was always no. Brackney smiled at the question and said, “The problem is that you have to pay it up front, and it’s hard to find that money.”

It would be easy, she said, to invoice the city for the classes instead of the student. It’s a simple answer, and one that would have seemed insurmountable even a few days ago.

In addition, changes can be made to which officers get access to valuable training, because in the past it has often been a case of who knew the right people. Each officer should have access to training that helps them in their discipline, she said.

She spoke of the men and women of JPD as people who could use a little humanizing, and she gave them a real belief that she saw them where they sat, not as a sea of blue uniforms.

The officers in that room seemed not so exhausted, not so tentative, and maybe even a little excited about what was to come as they left the meeting.

RELATED: What are Dr. Brackney’s qualifications?

Brackney then sat down with Darkhorse Press just before continuing for the rest of her packed Tuesday afternoon schedule.

Brackney is set to be confirmed at 4:30 p.m. today as the next chief of the Jackson Police Department, bringing with her a 40-year career in law enforcement and a vision rooted in community trust, officer wellness and faith.

She said she was drawn to Jackson not by chance, but by what she calls alignment.

When an alert crossed her phone announcing the position was open, she turned to her husband, who is from the area, and said, “Babe, Jackson is hiring.”

The couple had recently led a team to Jackson to explore a potential social justice impact project. The timing, she said, felt like divine intervention.

“There’s no reason that we led a team down here to do this work, and then this position opens up,” Brackney said. “This is aligning for a reason.”

She sees Jackson as uniquely positioned in American history as an epicenter of the civil rights movement and a city where policing once “got it wrong” in the assassination of Medgar Evers. Now, she said, there is an opportunity to build a department grounded in legitimacy, partnership, and prevention.

“Imagine if we can now think about public safety in a way that honors everyone,” she said. “That we build a department that’s filled with trust, filled with legitimacy, and is an actual real partner in community.”

Brackney rejects framing her leadership around being a woman in policing, noting she has spent four decades in uniform.

“I’ve been doing this for 40 years, and I’ve been a woman the whole time I’ve been doing it,” she said.

Instead, she focuses on what she calls relational policing as a practical benefit to officers.

“If you engage in these practices in an authentic way, you’re more likely to solve crimes, more likely to prevent crimes, more likely to be safer yourself,” she said, adding that it’s about knowing your community in a personal way, which leads to better communication and understanding.

Her philosophy is shaped by her own early experience in law enforcement. When she began her career at 21, she said she was often reduced to her race and gender. A mentor who looked beyond those demographics and invested in her potential changed the trajectory of her career.

“That changed the way I went about my work every single day,” she said. “If I can see the whole person behind the uniform, and that person is whole and healthy, the way they serve the community will be very different.”

Brackney also made clear that her approach to specialized units, including SWAT, will center on culture and accountability rather than a unit mentality.

“If you are engaging in a culture of corruption, I don’t care what unit you’re in,” she said. “You will no longer be there.”

Looking ahead five years, Brackney said her vision for the department is simple: joy.

“If you see them smiling, laughing when they come to work, enjoying coming to the space, and that joy translates into the community,” she said. “People don’t leave because of the work. They leave because of the organization and the leaders in it.”

Her goal, she said, is to reach a point where people line up to work for JPD, a sign that the department is moving in the right direction.

Brackney also spoke openly about the role faith plays in her leadership. Each morning, she begins with prayer and reflects on Psalm 78:72: “And David shepherded them with integrity of heart; with skillful hands he led them.”

“I always pray that God close any door He doesn’t want me to walk through,” she said. “In his heart a man plans his course, but God ordains his steps.”

As she prepares to formally take the helm, Brackney is asking for patience as she navigates the learning curve.

“Don’t underestimate the power of passion,” she said. “Give me a chance. Provide me with some grace.”

And, she added with a smile, recommendations for good places to eat in Jackson, because lingering in its places, listening to its people, and learning the city will be part of the work ahead.

Under previous administrations, reimbursement for related education was just something that was promised, but it was never given. The answer was always no. Brackney smiled at the question and said, “The problem is that you have to pay it up front, and it’s hard to find that money.”

It would be easy, she said, to invoice the city for the classes instead of the student. It’s a simple answer, and one that would have seemed insurmountable even a few days ago.

In addition, changes can be made to which officers get access to valuable training, because in the past it has often been a case of who knew the right people. Each officer should have access to training that helps them in their discipline, she said.

She spoke of the men and women of JPD as people who could use a little humanizing, and she gave them a real belief that she saw them where they sat, not as a sea of blue uniforms.

The officers in that room seemed not so exhausted, not so tentative, and maybe even a little excited about what was to come as they left the meeting.

RELATED: What are Dr. Brackney’s qualifications?

Brackney then sat down with Darkhorse Press just before continuing for the rest of her packed Tuesday afternoon schedule.

Brackney is set to be confirmed at 4:30 p.m. today as the next chief of the Jackson Police Department, bringing with her a 40-year career in law enforcement and a vision rooted in community trust, officer wellness and faith.

She said she was drawn to Jackson not by chance, but by what she calls alignment.

When an alert crossed her phone announcing the position was open, she turned to her husband, who is from the area, and said, “Babe, Jackson is hiring.”

The couple had recently led a team to Jackson to explore a potential social justice impact project. The timing, she said, felt like divine intervention.

“There’s no reason that we led a team down here to do this work, and then this position opens up,” Brackney said. “This is aligning for a reason.”

She sees Jackson as uniquely positioned in American history as an epicenter of the civil rights movement and a city where policing once “got it wrong” in the assassination of Medgar Evers. Now, she said, there is an opportunity to build a department grounded in legitimacy, partnership, and prevention.

“Imagine if we can now think about public safety in a way that honors everyone,” she said. “That we build a department that’s filled with trust, filled with legitimacy, and is an actual real partner in community.”

Brackney rejects framing her leadership around being a woman in policing, noting she has spent four decades in uniform.

“I’ve been doing this for 40 years, and I’ve been a woman the whole time I’ve been doing it,” she said.

Instead, she focuses on what she calls relational policing as a practical benefit to officers.

“If you engage in these practices in an authentic way, you’re more likely to solve crimes, more likely to prevent crimes, more likely to be safer yourself,” she said, adding that it’s about knowing your community in a personal way, which leads to better communication and understanding.

Her philosophy is shaped by her own early experience in law enforcement. When she began her career at 21, she said she was often reduced to her race and gender. A mentor who looked beyond those demographics and invested in her potential changed the trajectory of her career.

“That changed the way I went about my work every single day,” she said. “If I can see the whole person behind the uniform, and that person is whole and healthy, the way they serve the community will be very different.”

Brackney also made clear that her approach to specialized units, including SWAT, will center on culture and accountability rather than a unit mentality.

“If you are engaging in a culture of corruption, I don’t care what unit you’re in,” she said. “You will no longer be there.”

Looking ahead five years, Brackney said her vision for the department is simple: joy.

“If you see them smiling, laughing when they come to work, enjoying coming to the space, and that joy translates into the community,” she said. “People don’t leave because of the work. They leave because of the organization and the leaders in it.”

Her goal, she said, is to reach a point where people line up to work for JPD, a sign that the department is moving in the right direction.

Brackney also spoke openly about the role faith plays in her leadership. Each morning, she begins with prayer and reflects on Psalm 78:72: “And David shepherded them with integrity of heart; with skillful hands he led them.”

“I always pray that God close any door He doesn’t want me to walk through,” she said. “In his heart a man plans his course, but God ordains his steps.”

As she prepares to formally take the helm, Brackney is asking for patience as she navigates the learning curve.

“Don’t underestimate the power of passion,” she said. “Give me a chance. Provide me with some grace.”

And, she added with a smile, recommendations for good places to eat in Jackson, because lingering in its places, listening to its people, and learning the city will be part of the work ahead.

item.title: Daughtry to headline Pearl Day Concert on April 25 - Darkhorse Press
item.author: Morgan Howard
item.description:
PEARL – The City of Pearl announced that the multi-platinum rock band Daughtry will be performing for Pearl Day on April 25.

The band will play at the Trustmark Park North Parking Lot. The free community concert will begin with special guest “Hinder” at 7:00 p.m., followed by Daughtry taking the stage at 8:45 p.m.

One of the most visible and best-selling rock bands of the 21st century, Daughtry has sold out concerts across the globe. Their self-titled debut album, Daughtry , was the top-selling album of 2007 and the fastest-selling rock debut in Soundscan history. The album earned four GRAMMY® nominations and won four American Music Awards, along with seven Billboard Music Awards, including Album of the Year.

The band’s albums were: Leave This Town , Break The Spell , and Baptized. They all achieved Platinum status, while Cage To Rattle was certified Gold. In 2021, Daughtry released Dearly Beloved , producing multiple Top 10 singles. Their 2023 smash cover of “Separate Ways (Worlds Apart)” featuring Lzzy Hale marked another Top 10 success, followed by their return to rock roots with “Artificial,” their first No. 1 single in the Active Rock format, and the follow-up No. 1 hit, “Pieces.” Their newest EP, Shock to the System (Part One) , is available now on all platforms.

Joining the lineup is multi-platinum American rock band Hinder, whose debut album Extreme Behavior climbed to No. 6 on the Billboard 200 and has since been certified 3x Platinum. Their follow-up album, Take It To The Limit , peaked at No. 4 on the Billboard 200 and solidified the band’s presence on the global rock stage. Hinder’s latest album, Back To Life , continues to build momentum with their single “Bring Me Back To Life” climbing active rock charts worldwide.

“Bringing Daughtry to headline Pearl Day, with Hinder joining the lineup, is an incredible opportunity for our community,” said Mayor Jake Windham. “These nationally recognized rock bands will deliver a concert experience you’d typically only find in major markets, and we’re proud to provide it free for our residents and guests.”

The Pearl Day Concert will take place in the North Parking Lot of Trustmark Park on Saturday, April 25. Admission is free and anyone is invited to attend.

Don’t forget to bring a chair! No outside food or drinks are allowed.

item.title: UPDATE: Starkville officer remains in critical condition after funeral escort crash - Darkhorse Press
item.author: Morgan Howard
item.description:
STARKVILLE — A Starkville Police Department motorcycle officer remains in critical condition after being injured in a crash while assisting with a funeral procession escort, according to an update released Sunday by the department.

In the Feb. 23 update, police said they were following up after receiving strong community support and inquiries about the officer’s condition. The department said it is grateful for the “overwhelming support, prayers, and encouragement” from the local community, across Mississippi and from out of state.

Police said the officer remains in critical condition and asked the public to continue praying for the officer, the officer’s family and the Starkville Police Department.

According to the department’s original release, the crash happened Saturday, Feb. 21, at about 1:43 p.m. while the officer was assisting with a funeral procession escort.

Police said the department motorcycle and another vehicle, which was not part of the procession, collided. The officer suffered serious injuries and was flown to a medical facility in Jackson for treatment.

The driver of the other vehicle remained at the scene and declined medical treatment, police said.

The Starkville Police Department Accident Reconstruction Team is conducting a full reconstruction of the crash, and the collision remains under investigation.

item.title: STEPPING UP TO SERVE: MHP donates two patrol SUV's to Jefferson Davis County Sheriff's Department - Darkhorse Press
item.author: Morgan Howard
item.description:
JEFFERSON DAVIS COUNTY – Jefferson Davis County Sheriff Ron Strickland and the Jefferson Davis County Sheriff’s Office are thanking the Mississippi Highway Patrol and Trooper Victor Mays for donating two patrol SUVs to the department.

In a statement, the sheriff’s office said the donation will help deputies better serve and protect the community.

The department also thanked the Mississippi Highway Patrol for its continued support of public safety and for assisting fellow law enforcement agencies across the county and state.

Sheriff’s officials said partnerships between agencies help strengthen law enforcement efforts in Mississippi.

item.title: Zero-turn mower stolen from Seminary Youth Association Ball Field in Covington County - Darkhorse Press
item.author: Morgan Howard
item.description:
COVINGTON COUNTY — The Covington County Sheriff’s Office is asking for the public’s help identifying a vehicle and its occupants in connection with the theft of a 72-inch Big Dog zero-turn mower from the Seminary Youth Association Ball Field.

According to information released by the sheriff’s office, the theft occurred Feb. 19, 2026, at the ball field in Seminary.

Investigators said a vehicle arrived at the location at about 1:40 p.m. without a trailer, then returned at about 3:21 p.m. with a trailer. Authorities said the mower was stolen during the later visit.

The sheriff’s office is asking anyone who recognizes the vehicle or its occupants, or who saw the vehicle in the area, to contact its confidential tips line at 601-641-4618.

item.title: UMMC clinics to remain closed Monday, Tuesday - Darkhorse Press
item.author: Therese Apel
item.description:
By Patrice Guilfoyle University of Mississippi Medical Center

JACKSON – The University of Mississippi Medical Center clinics will remain closed statewide Monday and Tuesday.

Medical Center teams continue to respond to the Feb. 19 cyberattack alongside federal and state agencies and national experts in cybersecurity. Our phone systems and ability to receive or send emails remain down or unreliable.

The UMMC Emergency Departments and hospitals in Jackson, Madison County, Grenada and Holmes County are open and caring for patients. The Medical Center is actively working on a solution for patients who need to contact us with routine medical and/or medication needs.

UMMC has contacted patients receiving ongoing time-sensitive care to arrange treatment.

“Our care teams continue to provide exceptional service to our patients and families. I know this intrusion into our system has negatively impacted some of our patients, but please know that we are using every resource at our disposal to resolve this issue in a safe and effective manner,” said Dr. LouAnn Woodward, vice chancellor for health affairs.

item.title: Two Vicksburg Homicide Suspects Arrested in Indiana - Darkhorse Press
item.author: Therese Apel
item.description:
VICKSBURG — Two 18-year-old suspects wanted in connection with a Vicksburg homicide were arrested Sunday in Kokomo, Indiana, authorities said.

According to Kokomo police, officers were contacted by the Vicksburg Police Department on Feb. 21 regarding information that the suspects might be in the Kokomo area.

Investigators located the suspects’ vehicle at a residence east of the city. Around 8 a.m. Feb. 22, officers executed a search warrant at the home. After a brief negotiation, two suspects were taken into custody on first-degree murder charges.

Police identified the suspects as Quintin Wilson and Arkeyveion Stamps, both 18.

Vicksburg police said Wilson and Stamps are wanted in connection with the death of Jaylon Walker, 18, of Hermanville. Walker was found dead inside his vehicle on U.S. Highway 61 South in Vicksburg on Feb. 11.

Wilson and Stamps are being held in Indiana pending extradition to Mississippi.

item.title: State Fire Officials to Host Virtual Town Hall, Announce Major Funding for Departments - Darkhorse Press
item.author: Therese Apel
item.description:
JACKSON — Mississippi fire service leaders and personnel are invited to participate in a statewide virtual town hall Monday morning that will include major funding announcements, policy updates and a live question-and-answer session with program leaders.

The “MSFIRE Virtual Town Hall & Open Office Hours” is scheduled for 9 a.m. Feb. 23 and will be hosted by the state fire coordinator and MSFIRE. Organizers say the session is designed for fire chiefs, coordinators, officers, volunteers, career personnel and partner agencies across Mississippi.

Officials plan to release details during the session about millions of dollars in funding available for Mississippi fire departments.

The event will feature real-time statewide updates from the Mississippi Forestry Commission, the Mississippi Firefighters Association and state fire leadership, according to organizers.

Among the featured topics:

Organizers said additional statewide updates and open discussion will be included as time allows.

The virtual session is open to anyone connected to Mississippi’s fire service. Registration is available online.

item.title: Sheriff Urges Residents to Use Crime Stoppers to Help Fight Crime - Darkhorse Press
item.author: Therese Apel
item.description:
JACKSON — Jackson Police Chief and Hinds County Sheriff Tyree D. Jones is urging residents to report criminal activity through Central Mississippi Crime Stoppers, emphasizing that public safety depends on community involvement.

In a Facebook post late last week, Jones said fighting crime is not solely the responsibility of law enforcement but a shared duty among residents.

“The safety of our community is not the responsibility of law enforcement alone,” Jones said. “It belongs to all of us. Every tip called in, every piece of information shared, and every neighbor who speaks up makes our streets safer for the families we serve.”

Jones encouraged residents to use Central Mississippi Crime Stoppers, which allows individuals to submit tips anonymously.

“You do not have to give your name. You just have to care,” Jones said. “If you know something, say something. Together, we are stronger than crime.”

Crime Stoppers accepts tips by phone at 601-355-TIPS (8477) and online at p3tips.com .

item.title: Police Investigating Four Threats Against Pearl Daycares - Darkhorse Press
item.author: Therese Apel
item.description:
PEARL — Authorities are investigating four threats made against daycares in the city of Pearl since last week, officials said.

Investigators with the Pearl Police Department, working in coordination with the Mississippi Department of Homeland Security, are attempting to identify the source of the threats.

Police said they have not been able to verify the credibility of any of the threats at this time.

Out of an abundance of caution, both uniformed and non-uniformed Pearl police officers will increase patrols around local daycare centers to help ensure the safety of children and staff.

“The safety of our kids remains our top priority, and we will continue to take every necessary step to protect them,” officials said in a statement.

Authorities did not release additional details about the nature of the threats. The investigation remains ongoing.

item.title: Panola County Man Arrested in Armed Robbery at Dollar General - Darkhorse Press
item.author: Therese Apel
item.description:
PANOLA COUNTY — A 19-year-old man has been arrested in connection with an armed robbery at a Dollar General store in western Panola County, authorities said.

With assistance from Crime Stoppers of Panola County and the Quitman County Sheriff’s Office, investigators arrested Nequan Ballentine, 19, in connection with the robbery, according to the Panola County Sheriff’s Office.

The robbery occurred around 10 p.m. at the Dollar General located at McDowell Road and Highway 6. Authorities said an armed Black male wearing a hoodie and face covering entered the store at closing time and forced employees to open the safe. The suspect took money from the safe before fleeing in a gray-colored car westbound on Highway 6.

No injuries were reported.

Investigators initially asked anyone with information about the crime to contact Crime Stoppers of Panola County.

Ballentine’s charges and additional details about the arrest were not immediately released. The investigation remains ongoing.

item.title: Officials Urge Caution as Wildfires Burn Across South Mississippi - Darkhorse Press
item.author: Therese Apel
item.description:
PERRY COUNTY — Over the weekend, fire officials were urging residents to avoid outdoor burning and use extreme caution as dangerous fire conditions contributed to wildfires across south and central Mississippi.

No formal burn bans are currently in place, but the danger is real, authorities said.

A wildfire burned an estimated 500-acre area near Paret Tower Road in Perry County this weekend, authorities said. Law enforcement issued evacuation orders for nearby areas and assisted residents as flames threatened homes and other structures.

The wildfire comes as the National Weather Service warns that a combination of gusty winds, somewhat dry fuels and low relative humidity is creating significant fire danger. A Red Flag Warning remains in effect for portions of south Mississippi and northeast Louisiana along the Highway 84 corridor, where fuels are especially dry.

The Weather Service said open burning should not be attempted in the warning area because wildfires can spread rapidly. Farther north, officials said outdoor burning is discouraged because fires are more likely to escape control.

Fire service officials ask residents to use caution when burning debris and to pay close attention to weather conditions, noting that high winds can significantly increase the risk of losing control of a fire.

While there are currently no county-issued burn bans in effect, burn bans must be enacted by county boards of supervisors and are not automatically triggered when hazardous conditions develop.

Authorities said residents should not interpret the absence of a burn ban as an indication that conditions are safe. Instead, they are asking the public to avoid open burning during periods of high wind and low humidity and to follow evacuation orders and updates from local officials as conditions evolve.

Multiple agencies responded to the Perry County blaze, including the Mississippi Forestry Commission, the Beaumont Volunteer Fire Department and the Carnes Volunteer Fire Department, along with local law enforcement and other first responders.

Interstate 12 was shut down in both directions between Highway 59 and Highway 434 because of active forest fires.

The cause of the Perry County fire remains under investigation.

In Copiah County on Sunday morning, a debris burn grew out of control on Barnes Road, leading to a brush fire that destroyed about 50 acres of grass and brush, according to the Crystal Springs Volunteer Fire Department.

Officials told reporters the fire started around 10:30 a.m. when a homeowner was burning debris. High winds quickly pushed the flames beyond the homeowner’s control.

item.title: MSU hosts institute students helping solve statewide, global challenges - Darkhorse Press
item.author: Therese Apel
item.description:
STARKVILLE — High school students from across the Magnolia State convened at Mississippi State Friday as participants of the eighth-annual World Food Prize Mississippi Youth Institute, hosted by MSU’s College of Agriculture and Life Sciences.

The Mississippi Youth Institute provides high school students the opportunity to research and suggest solutions for some of the most pressing global issues, including food security, water quality and health, said acting CALS Dean and institute leader Darrell Sparks.

“These are our future leaders, so it is important that they learn to problem solve and effectively communicate their ideas with others,” he said. “This program provides a constructive space for them to network with their peers as well as experts from industry and academia.”

The institute included the Charles E. Lindley Lecture featuring Ryn Laster, director of food safety and animal welfare at Cal-Maine Foods Inc. headquartered in Ridgeland. She is a four-time MSU graduate and has spent her career ensuring the highest standards in egg production, quality assurance and sustainable practices that support public health and industry integrity. Her presentation emphasized the critical roles of science, policy and leadership in strengthening food systems. After the presentation, students participated in preparing meal packs for distribution to state food pantries.

Participating students were recognized as Borlaug Scholars, with select students invited to participate in the Global Youth Institute in Iowa in October. Participants are eligible for CALS scholarships and can apply to the Borlaug Ruan International Internship and the USDA Wallace-Carver Fellowship. The World Food Prize Global Youth Institute was created by 1970 Nobel Peace Prize winner Norman Borlaug, who spent his life’s work finding solutions to feed a hungry world.

Last year, Greenville Christian School’s Jaquarius Harvey was selected to attend the GYI. He has participated in the program for three years and shared his experience with this year’s attendees.

“Even though I grew up in a rural community, I didn’t know how I could fit into agriculture,” he said. “World Food Prize has shown me how I can use my unique skills to help others. After graduation, I plan to obtain an engineering degree and apply it in agricultural development.”

He encouraged anyone interested in the program to “get outside your comfort zone.”

“I’ve been able to experience so many new things, like speaking in front of larger crowds and even flying for the first time,” he said. “You never know what opportunities might come your way.”

This year’s World Food Prize Mississippi Youth Institute Borlaug Scholars are (by school):

The Mississippi Youth Institute is hosted by MSU with generous support from CALS alumni Barry L. and Lana B. White and the Madison Charitable Foundation. To learn more, visit www.worldfoodprize.org/en/youth_programs/global_youth_institute/mississippi.

For more on MSU’s College of Agriculture and Life Sciences, visit www.cals.msstate.edu .

Mississippi State University is taking care of what matters. Learn more at www.msstate.edu .
item.ns1:encoded:
STARKVILLE — High school students from across the Magnolia State convened at Mississippi State Friday as participants of the eighth-annual World Food Prize Mississippi Youth Institute, hosted by MSU’s College of Agriculture and Life Sciences.

The Mississippi Youth Institute provides high school students the opportunity to research and suggest solutions for some of the most pressing global issues, including food security, water quality and health, said acting CALS Dean and institute leader Darrell Sparks.

“These are our future leaders, so it is important that they learn to problem solve and effectively communicate their ideas with others,” he said. “This program provides a constructive space for them to network with their peers as well as experts from industry and academia.”

The institute included the Charles E. Lindley Lecture featuring Ryn Laster, director of food safety and animal welfare at Cal-Maine Foods Inc. headquartered in Ridgeland. She is a four-time MSU graduate and has spent her career ensuring the highest standards in egg production, quality assurance and sustainable practices that support public health and industry integrity. Her presentation emphasized the critical roles of science, policy and leadership in strengthening food systems. After the presentation, students participated in preparing meal packs for distribution to state food pantries.

Participating students were recognized as Borlaug Scholars, with select students invited to participate in the Global Youth Institute in Iowa in October. Participants are eligible for CALS scholarships and can apply to the Borlaug Ruan International Internship and the USDA Wallace-Carver Fellowship. The World Food Prize Global Youth Institute was created by 1970 Nobel Peace Prize winner Norman Borlaug, who spent his life’s work finding solutions to feed a hungry world.

Last year, Greenville Christian School’s Jaquarius Harvey was selected to attend the GYI. He has participated in the program for three years and shared his experience with this year’s attendees.

“Even though I grew up in a rural community, I didn’t know how I could fit into agriculture,” he said. “World Food Prize has shown me how I can use my unique skills to help others. After graduation, I plan to obtain an engineering degree and apply it in agricultural development.”

He encouraged anyone interested in the program to “get outside your comfort zone.”

“I’ve been able to experience so many new things, like speaking in front of larger crowds and even flying for the first time,” he said. “You never know what opportunities might come your way.”

This year’s World Food Prize Mississippi Youth Institute Borlaug Scholars are (by school):

The Mississippi Youth Institute is hosted by MSU with generous support from CALS alumni Barry L. and Lana B. White and the Madison Charitable Foundation. To learn more, visit www.worldfoodprize.org/en/youth_programs/global_youth_institute/mississippi.

For more on MSU’s College of Agriculture and Life Sciences, visit www.cals.msstate.edu .

Mississippi State University is taking care of what matters. Learn more at www.msstate.edu .

item.title: Monday Weather: Fire danger today! - Darkhorse Press
item.author: Mary Apel
item.description:
The combination of breezy northwest winds, somewhat dry fuels, and low relative humidity values will lead to significant fire danger concerns persisting today.

After a chilly start to the week, another warm up is expected mid & late week, with a cold front currently forecast to affect the area Thursday. Quiet weather exists through Wednesday, but a cold front brings increasing chances for rain Thursday into Thursday night, with quieter conditions expected Friday into the weekend.

item.title: MDOC inmates complete financial literacy boot camp at Madison County work center - Darkhorse Press
item.author: Morgan Howard
item.description:
MADISON COUNTY — The Mississippi Department of Corrections said inmates at the Madison County Community Work Center recently completed a financial literacy boot camp aimed at helping participants build money management skills.

MDOC said the class was offered through a partnership with the Mississippi Community Financial Access Coalition and Guaranty Bank.

The department said 24 participants took part in the program. Inmate Earvin Boglin told fellow participants that saving money requires time, self-discipline and patience, and said he learned the importance of avoiding high-cost borrowing such as payday loans.

MDOC also said inmate Colby Reed encouraged fellow inmates to stay positive and keep faith while working toward a better future.

The department said several participants, including Boglin and Reed, were featured in photos with community work center staff following the class.

item.title: MDAC Accepting Proposals for Specialty Crop Block Grant Program - Darkhorse Press
item.author: Therese Apel
item.description:
JACKSON — The Mississippi Department of Agriculture and Commerce is accepting proposals for the Specialty Crop Block Grant Program, Commissioner Andy Gipson announced.

The program, funded by the U.S. Department of Agriculture and authorized by the Agriculture Improvement Act of 2018, aims to enhance the competitiveness of Mississippi’s specialty crop industry.

The grants support efforts to market and promote specialty crops, assist producers with research and development, expand access and availability, and address local, regional and national challenges facing specialty crop producers.

Specialty crops include fruits and vegetables, tree nuts, dried fruits, horticulture and nursery crops, including floriculture. According to MDAC, specialty crops accounted for more than $201 million of Mississippi’s agriculture industry in 2025.

Past projects funded through the program have included garden grants to increase awareness and consumption of specialty crops among school-aged children; marketing campaigns promoting locally grown products; food safety improvements through Good Agricultural Practices; research into new crop varieties; honey bee colony health initiatives; grower education seminars and trainings; optical imaging technology for sweet potato and blueberry packing lines; and high tunnel research trials for fruits, vegetables and herbs.

Eligible applicants include producer groups and associations, nonprofits, colleges and universities. Funding will not be awarded for projects that solely benefit a single commercial product or provide profit to one organization, institution or individual, though individual entities may participate as project partners.

Applications must be submitted to MDAC by April 1, 2026. Application materials are available in the “Alerts and Announcements” section of mdac.ms.gov, by calling Susan Lawrence at 601-213-7542 or by emailing [email protected] .

item.title: Man Fatally Shot During Domestic Disturbance on Lake Glen Drive, Police Say - Darkhorse Press
item.author: Therese Apel
item.description:
JACKSON — A 35-year-old man was fatally shot early Sunday during a reported domestic disturbance at a residence in Jackson, authorities said.

Officers with the Jackson Police Department’s Precinct 3 responded about 7:21 a.m. to the 600 block of Lake Glen Drive after receiving reports of a disturbance. When officers arrived, they learned that shots had been fired at the home.

Inside the residence, officers found the body of a man lying at the front door of the house. He was pronounced dead at the scene.

Police later identified the victim as Michael D. Monger, 35. His next of kin has been notified.

Several individuals were inside the home at the time of the shooting, according to police. Weapons and shell casings were recovered from the scene.

The department’s Robbery/Homicide Unit and Mobile Crime Unit responded to assist with the investigation, along with the Hinds County Sheriff’s Office coroner.

Multiple people were transported to Jackson Police Department headquarters for questioning by homicide detectives. As of Sunday, no arrests had been made.

The investigation remains ongoing. Anyone with information is asked to contact the Jackson Police Department at 601-960-1234 or Crime Stoppers at 601-355-TIPS (8477).

item.title: Early Morning Fire Damages Units at Walker Hill Apartments in Meridian - Darkhorse Press
item.author: Cash Matlock
item.description:
MERIDIAN – A structure fire early Monday morning damaged multiple units at the Walker Hill Apartments in Meridian, according to fire officials.

The fire was reported around 5 a.m. at the apartment complex located near 21st Street and Old Marion Road.

Meridian Fire Chief Michael Evans said one unit was heavily damaged by the blaze, and the roof of a neighboring unit also sustained damage.

Firefighters evacuated residents from surrounding apartments as a precaution. No injuries were reported.

Officials said the fire is believed to have started in a bedroom. The cause remains under investigation.

item.title: Community diaper drive planned March 14 in Booneville - Darkhorse Press
item.author: Morgan Howard
item.description:
BOONEVILLE – The Northeast Mississippi Healthy Start Program and Consortium, in collaboration with the Mississippi State University Extension Center, will host a Community Diaper Drive on Saturday, March 14, in Booneville.

The event is scheduled for 10 a.m. to 2 p.m. at the Prentiss County Agri-Center, located at 2301 North Second Street.

Organizers said the event will provide free diapers, wipes and hygiene essentials to the community. A crib raffle also is planned.

For more information, organizers listed Katherine Hickman and Ray Shoemaker as contacts. Email contacts on the flyer are [email protected] and [email protected] .
